speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit off lately, especially in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in today. Can you tell me more about what’s been going on? Any specific symptoms or changes you've noticed?

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your vitals — your heart rate is 73 bpm, which is within normal range.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve had dizziness — especially when standing up quickly. It’s happened a few times this week.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important. Let’s review your current medications and recent labs. You’re currently on Amlodipine for blood pressure control.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Amlodipine 5mg once daily to help manage your hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure today is 88 mmHg — slightly elevated but not alarming yet.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 140 g/L — solid, no anemia concerns.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history, lab trends, and clinical presentation, your primary diagnosis remains Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose today is 10.5 mmol/L — above target, suggesting suboptimal glycemic control.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, let me recheck your blood pressure — this time it's showing a diastolic of 73 mmHg, quite a drop from the earlier reading.

speaker: patient
text: That’s strange — I did feel lightheaded right before that second reading. Maybe it’s related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — orthostatic changes could explain both the dizziness and the BP fluctuation. Let’s monitor that closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin has trended upward — today’s value is 153 g/L, which is higher than your previous result.

speaker: doctor
text: Wait — actually, let me correct that: your most recent lab report shows hemoglobin at 153 g/L, but I see your baseline was 140 g/L. That’s a meaningful increase.

speaker: patient
text: I haven’t had any transfusions or iron supplements — could dehydration be affecting these numbers?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s a good point — hemoconcentration could elevate hemoglobin. Let’s also assess hydration status and renal function.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate just spiked to 103 bpm — notably higher than the 73 we saw earlier. Are you feeling anxious or short of breath right now?

speaker: patient
text: A little shaky, yes — and my palms are sweaty. That reading feels different from the one you mentioned earlier — the one at 73 bpm.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your elevated glucose, recurrent dizziness, and hemodynamic variability, I’m going to start Metformin 1000mg twice daily to improve glycemic control and reduce cardiovascular strain. And just to clarify — your hemoglobin remains stable at 140 g/L per your last confirmed draw, so the 153 may reflect transient factors.
